bigQuestion: How are animals different from one another?

words.definition_en: Outer layer covering your whole body.
Animals that have fur and feed milk.
Round or oval object babies grow inside.
Animals that live in water and land.
Small hard flat pieces covering fish bodies.
Body parts fish use to breathe underwater.
Light fluffy pieces covering bird bodies.
Body parts birds use to fly high.
Soft thick hair covering some animals.

examples: Fish have gills. They have gills. Gills help them breathe.
Do gills help fish breathe? Yes, gills help them breathe.
How do gills help fish? Gills help them breathe.
rules: Use subject pronouns (I, you, he, she, it, we, they) as the subject of a sentence.
Use object pronouns (me, you, him, her, it, us, them) as the object of a verb.
Subject pronouns replace the noun doing the action; object pronouns replace the noun receiving the action.

bigQuestion: How are animals different from one another?

words.definition_en: Small animal that lives in soil.
Small sweet fruits that grow on plants.
To search for and catch animals.
To get away from a dangerous place.
To move slowly and quietly along ground.
To struggle against someone or something.
When birds hit with their sharp beaks.
Brown seed container from pine trees.
Loud harsh cry that birds make.

examples: Lily always escapes.
Lily is usually very nervous.
Leo sometimes tries to catch Lily.
Leo hardly ever tries to catch Lily now.
Leo and Lily never fight now.
rules: Adverbs of frequency tell how often something happens.
always = 100%, usually = ~75%, sometimes = ~50%, hardly ever = ~10%, never = 0%
Adverbs of frequency go before the main verb but after the verb 'to be'.

topic: What Is Our World Made Of?

words.definition_en: To move smoothly like water.
Hard material that keeps its shape.
Wet substance that pours and flows.
Invisible air that floats around.
Warmth that makes things hot.
Hot invisible gas from boiling water.
Frozen water that is hard and cold.
To turn liquid into hard ice.
To turn solid ice into liquid water.

grammar.point: Simple Past of Verb To Be (Statements)
examples: It was a liquid. Now it's a gas.
They were hard. Now they're soft.
It wasn't small, it was very tall.
Those were his black eyes.
rules: Use 'was' with I, he, she, it for simple past of 'to be'.
Use 'were' with you, we, they for simple past of 'to be'.
For negatives, use wasn't (was not) or weren't (were not).

words.definition_en: Thick white liquid from milk.
Sweet white crystals that taste good.
White crystals that make food salty.
Let liquid flow from container.
Thin containers made from plastic material.
Things stirred together in one bowl.
Not closed, you can go inside.
Shut tight, nothing can go in.
Very cold box that keeps food frozen.

grammar.point: Simple Past of Verb To Be (Questions)
examples: Was the freezer door open? Yes, it was.
Were the students happy? No, they weren't.
Where was the ice cream? It was in the freezer.
rules: To make a yes/no question, put 'was' or 'were' before the subject.
To make a Wh- question, put the question word before 'was' or 'were'.
Short answers: Yes, it was. / No, it wasn't. Yes, they were. / No, they weren't.

bigQuestion: How are things different now from long ago?

words.definition_en: Going from one place to another place.
words.definition_zh: 從一個地方去另一個地方

words.definition_en: Sharing information and ideas with other people.
words.definition_zh: 與他人分享信息和想法

words.definition_en: A big machine that flies through the sky.
words.definition_zh: 在天空中飛行的大機器

words.definition_en: Information about things that happened recently.
words.definition_zh: 最近發生的事情的信息

words.definition_en: Words written on paper and sent by mail.
words.definition_zh: 寫在紙上通過郵件寄送的文字

words.definition_en: A short written message sent on a phone.
words.definition_zh: 用手機發送的短文字信息

words.definition_en: Messages sent through a computer or phone.
words.definition_zh: 通過電腦或手機發送的信息

words.definition_en: A machine that plays music and news sounds.
words.definition_zh: 播放音樂和新聞聲音的機器

words.definition_en: A system connecting computers around the world.
words.definition_zh: 連接世界各地電腦的系統

examples: People listened to the news on the radio.
They didn't watch the news on TV.
People traveled by horse and cart.
They didn't travel by airplane.
rules: Add -ed to regular verbs to make the simple past: listen → listened, travel → traveled.
For negatives, use didn't + base form of the verb.
The base form stays the same for all subjects with didn't.

bigQuestion: How are things different now from long ago?

words.definition_en: Come to a place at last.
Question someone to get answer.
Look at something carefully.
Go into a place or room.
Not having much money or things.
Too many people in one place.
Person who helps customers in store.
Go to see someone or somewhere.
Not feeling well in your body.

examples: Did they travel by ship? Yes, they did.
Did they arrive in two days? No, they didn't.
What did they do? They lived with Carlo's uncle.
rules: To make a yes/no question in simple past, use did + subject + base form.
To make a Wh- question, use the question word + did + subject + base form.
Short answers: Yes, they did. / No, they didn't.

words.definition_en: A symbol that means take away or subtract.
Taking away numbers to find the answer.
To remove or subtract one number from another.
Numbers or things arranged up and down.
Numbers or things arranged left to right.
The number of points you get on a test.
A number from zero to nine only.
A number from ten to ninety-nine.
The direction opposite from your right.

examples: Alma bought five cookies. She didn't buy four.
Olivia gave her sister twenty stickers.
She didn't give her twenty-six.
rules: Some verbs have irregular past forms: eat → ate, see → saw, buy → bought, give → gave.
For negatives with irregular verbs, use didn't + the base form (not the irregular form).
You must memorize irregular past tense forms — they don't follow a pattern.

words.definition_en: Release something you are holding.
Take something that is not yours.
Remove something from under ground.
Put something where no one can see.
Container with a wide mouth and lid.
Wanting more than you really need.
Not interested or feeling tired and dull.
Person who makes bread and cakes.
Prepare food using heat for eating.

examples: Did Bandar go to a candy store? Yes, he did.
Did he let go of the candy? No, he didn't.
What did Bandar do with the cookies? He ate them.
rules: To make yes/no questions with irregular past verbs, use did + subject + base form.
To make Wh- questions, use question word + did + subject + base form.
Key irregular verbs: make → made, buy → bought, take → took, sell → sold, go → went.

bigQuestion: How do people get along with each other?

words.definition_en: Colored lights that tell cars when to stop.
words.definition_zh: 告訴汽車何時停止的彩色燈

words.definition_en: Each person does something one after another.
Being kind and respectful to other people.
To make a place neat and free of mess.
Trash or garbage left on the ground.
Person who works in a library and helps find books.
words.definition_zh: 在圖書館工作幫助尋找書籍的人

words.definition_en: Adult who helps children safely cross the street.
words.definition_zh: 幫助兒童安全過馬路的成人

words.definition_en: The leader in charge of a school.
Person who watches swimmers and keeps them safe.
words.definition_zh: 監視游泳者並保護他們安全的人

examples: Those are your things. Those things are yours.
They aren't my toys. They aren't mine.
Whose book is this? It's hers.
Whose pencils are these? They're his.
rules: Possessive pronouns replace a possessive adjective + noun: my book → mine, your book → yours.
Possessive pronouns: mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs.
Use 'Whose' to ask who something belongs to.

topic: The Please and Thank You Book of Poems
bigQuestion: How do people get along with each other?

words.definition_en: Tap on door to get someone's attention.
Thinking about how to help others.
Not being polite or kind to people.
Quickly take something with your hand.
Give something to another person.
Move something to its proper place.
Ask someone to come to event.
Clean something with water and soap.
Give some of yours to someone else.

examples: Can I play? Yes, you can. / No, you can't.
May I come in? Yes, you may. / No, you may not.
rules: Use 'can' to ask if something is possible or to ask for permission informally.
Use 'may' to ask for permission more formally and politely.
Negative: can't / cannot; may not.

bigQuestion: Why should we take care of the Earth?

words.definition_en: Things we use that come from nature.
Hard material that comes from trees.
Place where people put garbage and trash.
Ground and soil where things grow.
Garbage or things we throw away.
Gray cloud that comes from fire.
Light and heat that comes from sun.
Use less of something to help earth.
Use something again instead of throwing.

examples: There's smoke in the air above the city.
The landfill is across from the park.
The trash can is behind the tree.
The tree is in front of the trash can.
rules: Use 'above' to mean higher than something.
Use 'across from' to mean on the other side.
Use 'behind' to mean at the back of, and 'in front of' to mean at the front of.

bigQuestion: Why should we take care of the Earth?

words.definition_en: A box made from thick paper.
A book where you write daily thoughts.
Thin material used for writing on.
To push air out of your mouth hard.
A flat board for holding things up.
A place that turns old things into new things.
A thing that does work using power.
A shop that sells pens and paper.
To save someone from danger or trouble.

examples: Where's the fountain? It's across from the statue.
Is there a trash can behind the tree? Yes, there is.
rules: Use 'Where' + is/are to ask about location.
Use 'Is there / Are there' + preposition phrase to ask yes/no location questions.
Short answers: Yes, there is. / No, there isn't. Yes, there are. / No, there aren't.

bigQuestion: How does music make us feel?

words.definition_en: Very far up in the air or sky.
Not far up; close to the ground.
Very happy and full of energy.
words.definition_zh: 興奮的；充滿能量和快樂

words.definition_en: Feeling tired and wanting to sleep.
A wild animal like a big dog.
Emotions you have inside your heart.
Correct; the opposite of left.
Not correct; not the right answer.
A musical instrument you blow into.

examples: Pedro listens to slow music at night.
When does Amanda listen to slow music? She listens in the morning.
rules: Use 'at' with specific times: at night, at 7 o'clock, at noon.
Use 'in' with parts of the day (except night): in the morning, in the afternoon, in the evening.
Use 'on' with days: on Monday, on Saturday.

bigQuestion: How does music make us feel?

words.definition_en: Written words or musical sounds.
Stay in one place until something happens.
words.definition_zh: 留在一個地方直到發生某事

words.definition_en: Feeling nervous or scared about something.
words.definition_zh: 對某事感到緊張或害怕

words.definition_en: A sickness that makes you feel very sick.
words.definition_zh: 使你感到非常生病的疾病

words.definition_en: Water drops that fall from your eyes.
Performing or doing something all by yourself.
Tiny bird that flies very fast and drinks nectar.
words.definition_zh: 飛得很快並喝花蜜的小鳥

words.definition_en: Write down information to save it for later.
words.definition_zh: 寫下信息以便稍後保存

words.definition_en: Person who plays music or sings songs.

examples: This morning, Olga goes to school early for extra practice.
When did Mark go to music class? He went yesterday morning.
rules: Adverbs of time tell when an action happens: this morning, yesterday, last week, last night.
Adverbs of time can go at the beginning or end of a sentence.
Use 'When' to ask about the time of an action.

words.definition_en: Use force to move something away.
Use force to move something toward you.
When something changes position or location.
The earth or surface you stand on.
Use your arm to send something through air.
How fast something is moving or going.
Weighs a lot and is hard to lift.
Does not weigh very much at all.
Not hard to do or understand well.

examples: A ball is lighter than a desk.
Is a desk heavier than a ball? Yes, it is.
What's slower than an airplane? A train is slower than an airplane.
rules: Add -er to short adjectives to compare two things: fast → faster, slow → slower.
For adjectives ending in consonant + vowel + consonant, double the final consonant: big → bigger.
For adjectives ending in -y, change y to i and add -er: heavy → heavier.

words.definition_en: Refusing to change your mind or opinion.
Feeling very upset and mad inside.
Farm animal with horns and a beard.
Very tall natural hill made of rock.
Direction where the sun goes down.
Direction where the sun comes up.
Moving toward the front direction ahead.
Hard pointed parts on animal heads.
Covered or soaked with water.

examples: I'm the youngest person in my family.
Is the juiciest grass on East Mountain? Yes, it is.
rules: Add -est to short adjectives to make the superlative: young → youngest, loud → loudest.
For adjectives ending in -y, change y to i and add -est: juicy → juiciest.
Use 'the' before superlative adjectives.

words.definition_en: Curved shape like the moon.
words.definition_zh: 像月亮一样的弯曲形状

words.definition_en: Bright light in the night sky.
Circular pattern that winds around.
Shape like an egg or circle.
Line that does not bend or curve.
All plants, animals, and outdoors.
Artwork made from cut pieces.
words.definition_zh: 用剪下的碎片制作的艺术作品

words.definition_en: 3D artwork carved or shaped.
words.definition_zh: 雕刻或塑造的立体艺术作品

words.definition_en: Design that repeats over again.

examples: There are a lot of shapes.
There aren't any triangles.
There is a lot of paper.
There isn't any red paint.
rules: Use 'a lot of' with both countable and uncountable nouns in positive sentences.
Use 'any' with countable and uncountable nouns in negative sentences.
Use 'are' with plural countable nouns; use 'is' with uncountable nouns.

words.definition_en: Bright and reflecting light like a mirror.
Bend paper or cloth in half neatly.
The outer border or side of something.
A tall bird with long legs and neck.
A sea animal with flippers and fur.
Squeeze something into wrinkles and bumps.
Water moving up and down in ocean.
Go up something using hands and feet.
Having the bright yellow color of gold.

examples: Did Maki have any pencils? No, she didn't.
How many seals were there? There was one seal.
How much paper was there? There was a lot of paper.
rules: Use 'any' in questions with both countable and uncountable nouns.
Use 'How many' to ask about countable nouns.
Use 'How much' to ask about uncountable nouns.
